perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement: (a) “Affiliates ” mean s entities controlling, controlled by or under common control with Vendor or with Company.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement: (a) “Affiliates ” means entities with over 50% ownership by Vendor or with Company. For the purposes of data sharing agreements within this NDA, "Affiliates" includes any entity with which Vendor or Company conducts regular business.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction in the definition of 'Affiliates.' The initial definition is based on control, while the modified definition introduces a specific ownership percentage for general purposes, but expands it for data sharing contexts. This creates uncertainty. The first definition is specific to 50% ownership, but the second definition could include any entities with regular business even with less than 50% ownership, leading to confusion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party may disclose the Con fidential Information to the employ- ees, officers, directors, and Affiliates of Receiving Party who need to know such Confi- dential Information in connection with the Purpose and who receive such information subject to the same or comparable restrictions as are contained in this Agreement as evi- denced by a signed non -disclosure agreement or equivalent . Disclosure of the Confiden- tial Information to outside agents and affiliates other than Receiving Party must be agreed upon by the Disclosing Party in writing .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Affiliates.' The original definition relies on control, whereas the changed definition introduces an ownership percentage criterion for general use, but then expands the definition to include entities with regular business relationships for data sharing contexts. This creates an in-text contradiction. The contradicted text discusses disclosing Confidential Information to Affiliates, which is defined in the Changed Text differently based on the context. The different definitions create a contradiction and makes enforcement unclear, because it isn't clear who qualifies as an Affiliate in the Contradicted Text.

perturbation.original_text: (b) “Confidential Information ” mean s: (1) any information, whether tangi- ble or intangible, in written or in machine readable form that is mar ked or designated in writing as “Proprietary” or “Confidential” at the time of disclosure ; (2) any information disclosed orally or visually to Receiving P arty, provided that such information is orally identified as “Confidential” or “Proprietary” prior to or at the time of its disclosure , (3) any information derived or extracted from information provided to Receiving Party that Receiving Party reasonably should conclude was intended to be confidential, or (4) any information that is considered a trade secre t and not expressly released to Receiving Party by Disclosing Party .
perturbation.changed_text: (b) “Confidential Information ” means: (1) any information, whether tangi- ble or intangible, in written or in machine readable form that is marked or designated in writing as “Proprietary” or “Confidential” at the time of disclosure; (2) any information disclosed orally or visually to Receiving Party, provided that such information is orally identified as “Confidential” or “Proprietary” prior to or at the time of its disclosure; (3) any information derived or extracted from information provided to Receiving Party regardless of whether Receiving Party reasonably should conclude it was intended to be confidential, or (4) any information that is considered a trade secret and not expressly released to Receiving Party by Disclosing Party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, information provided by the Vendor is only considered "Confidential Information" if expressly agreed to in writing by both parties within 30 days of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding the definition of 'Confidential Information' and what constitutes as such. Initially, derived information needed to be reasonably concluded as confidential, now is regardless of whether Receiving Party reasonably should conclude it was intended to be confidential. However, a new clause states that Vendor's information requires express written agreement, contradicting the general definition and creating ambiguity regarding what constitutes Vendor's confidential information. The new clause creates an exception specifically for the Vendor, and will contradict the general definition. The first section includes that regardless of Receiving Party conclude, should be considered. The second section specifically makes an exception for the Vendor.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement is and shall remain the property of Disclo sing Party. Receiving Party shall not alter or remove any confidentiality or proprietary marking on the Confidential Information and , subject to the forego- ing, may make such limited number of copies of Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the ex tent necessary to achieve the Purpose. Within 15 days of written request from Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall promptly return Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof to Disclosing Party or destroy Disclosing Party’s Conf idential Information, in- cluding all copies thereof, purge its computer systems of such Confidential Information and cer- tify such destruction in writing signed by a duly authorized representative . Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may retain o ne copy in a confidential file for archival purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a new condition for Vendor's confidential information, requiring express written agreement within 30 days. This directly contradicts the contradicted text which states that 'All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement is and shall remain the property of Disclosing Party'. The original text's definition of confidential information included information derived or extracted regardless of the Receiving Party's conclusion, but now Vendor information has an exception. This exception creates a direct conflict and ambiguity on how Vendor's information is handled as Confidential Information, and creates an ambiguity, which aligns with the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party may disclose the Con fidential Information to the employ- ees, officers, directors, and Affiliates of Receiving Party who need to know such Confi- dential Information in connection with the Purpose and who receive such information subject to the same or comparable restrictions as are contained in this Agreement as evi- denced by a signed non -disclosure agreement or equivalent . Disclosure of the Confiden- tial Information to outside agents and affiliates other than Receiving Party must be agreed upon by the Disclosing Party in writing ..
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to the employees, officers, and directors of Receiving Party who need to know such Confidential Information in connection with the Purpose and who receive such information subject to the same or comparable restrictions as are contained in this Agreement as evidenced by a signed non-disclosure agreement or equivalent. Disclosure of the Confidential Information to Affiliates and outside agents must be agreed upon by the Disclosing Party in writing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "Affiliates" from the initial list of entities to which Receiving Party can disclose Confidential Information without prior consent, and instead including them in the subsequent clause requiring Disclosing Party's written agreement, a contradiction is created. The initial text suggests Affiliates are treated similarly to employees, officers, and directors, while the modified text treats them like outside agents, needing Disclosing Party's explicit consent. This introduces ambiguity about how Affiliates are to be handled regarding Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used in this Agreement: (a) “Affiliates ” mean s entities controlling, controlled by or under common control with Vendor or with Company. (b) “Confidential Information ” mean s: (1) any information, whether tangi- ble or intangible, in written or in machine readable form that is mar ked or designated in writing as “Proprietary” or “Confidential” at the time of disclosure ; (2) any information disclosed orally or visually to Receiving P arty, provided that such information is orally identified as “Confidential” or “Proprietary” prior to or at the time of its disclosure , (3) any information derived or extracted from information provided to Receiving Party that Receiving Party reasonably should conclude was intended to be confidential, or (4) any information that is considered a trade secre t and not expressly released to Receiving Party by Disclosing Party .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original text by removing 'Affiliates' from the list of entities to which Confidential Information can be disclosed without prior consent, and instead includes them in the clause requiring the Disclosing Party's written agreement. This contradicts the implied treatment of Affiliates in the original text, where they were treated similarly to employees, officers, and directors. The Contradicted Text defines Affiliates but does not suggest they should be treated differently from employees or directors in terms of confidential information. By now requiring written consent, it creates ambiguity about how Affiliates are handled which breaks the original implicit compatibility and creates a strong in-text contradiction, adhering to the 'Ambiguities' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall use at least the same degree of care, but in no event less than reasonable care, to avoid inadvertent disclosure or unpermitted use of the other par- ty's Confidential Information which it employs with respect to its own proprietar y or con- fidential information of a similar nature which it does not wish to have disseminated, published or disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall use a commercially reasonable degree of care to avoid inadvertent disclosure or unpermitted use of the other party's Confidential Information which it employs with respect to its own proprietary or confidential information of a similar nature which it does not wish to have disseminated, published or disclosed. For avoidance of doubt, "commercially reasonable degree of care" shall not require implementing security measures beyond those required by applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The change alters the standard of care required to protect Confidential Information. The original text sets a baseline of 'reasonable care,' ensuring a consistent level of protection. The modified version uses 'commercially reasonable degree of care,' which is further qualified to not exceed legal requirements. This introduces an internal contradiction because the parties' existing security practices might fall short of 'reasonable care' and now there are two ways to measure it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The restrictions and obligations set forth in this Agreement shall survive any expiration or termination for five years from the date of such expiration or termination with respect to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement prior to th e date of such expiration or termination .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the standard of care for protecting confidential information from 'at least the same degree of care, but in no event less than reasonable care' to 'commercially reasonable degree of care'. The added qualification that this doesn't require measures beyond legal requirements creates a potential situation where legally compliant security is less than 'reasonable care'. This contradicts the contradicted text because the survival clause expects a consistent level of protection for confidential information, but the new standard allows for potentially weaker protection, rendering the continued obligation ambiguous and potentially unenforceable at the 'reasonable care' standard expected originally.

perturbation.original_text: Within 15 days of written request from Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall promptly return Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof to Disclosing Party or destroy Disclosing Party’s Conf idential Information, in- cluding all copies thereof, purge its computer systems of such Confidential Information and cer- tify such destruction in writing signed by a duly authorized representative . Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may retain o ne copy in a confidential file for archival purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Within 15 days of written request from Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall use reasonable efforts to return Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof to Disclosing Party or destroy Disclosing Party’s Conf idential Information, in- cluding all copies thereof, purge its computer systems of such Confidential Information and cer- tify such destruction in writing signed by a duly authorized representative. Receiving Party may retain one copy in a confidential file for archival purposes. Upon expiration or termination of this agreement the Receiving Party is no longer required to return information but only use reasonable efforts.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall promptly return' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to return', the obligation becomes less stringent and introduces uncertainty. The addition of the termination clause adds a contradiction, that at termination of the agreement the receiving party is no longer required to return information. This contradicts the original text where regardless of termination the receiving party at 15 day notice, is required to return the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Dat e and shall expire three years’ thereafter, unless earlier terminated by either party upon at least ten days’ prior notice to the other par ty. The restrictions and obligations set forth in this Agreement shall survive any expiration or termination for five years from the date of such expiration or termination with respect to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement prior to th e date of such expiration or termination .
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return confidential information upon request, and introduces a condition that upon termination the receiving party is no longer required to return the information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the obligations set forth in the agreement, including those related to confidential information, survive any expiration or termination for five years. The changed text introduces an exception to the survival clause, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (a) The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Dat e and shall expire three years’ thereafter, unless earlier terminated by either party upon at least ten days’ prior notice to the other par ty. The restrictions and obligations set forth in this Agreement shall survive any expiration or termination for five years from the date of such expiration or termination with respect to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement prior to th e date of such expiration or termination .
perturbation.changed_text: (a) The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall expire three years’ thereafter, unless earlier terminated by either party upon at least ten days’ prior notice to the other party. The restrictions and obligations set forth in this Agreement shall survive any expiration or termination for five years, except that the obligation to maintain confidentiality shall continue indefinitely with respect to trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a conflict between the general survival clause and the special condition added. The original clause states that restrictions and obligations survive for five years. However, the amendment adds that the obligation to maintain confidentiality for trade secrets is indefinite. This creates a conflict: Does *all* information disclosed before termination/expiration adhere to the five-year limit, or do trade secrets have no limit, and which applies? This contradiction creates a conflict within the survival clause itself, leading to enforcement issues. The five year limitation contradicts the indefinetly
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used in this Agreement: (b) “Confidential Information ” mean s: (1) any information, whether tangi- ble or intangible, in written or in machine readable form that is mar ked or designated in writing as “Proprietary” or “Confidential” at the time of disclosure ; (2) any information disclosed orally or visually to Receiving P arty, provided that such information is orally identified as “Confidential” or “Proprietary” prior to or at the time of its disclosure , (3) any information derived or extracted from information provided to Receiving Party that Receiving Party reasonably should conclude was intended to be confidential, or (4) any information that is considered a trade secre t and not expressly released to Receiving Party by Disclosing Party .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the survival clause by stating that confidentiality obligations for trade secrets continue indefinitely, contradicting the original five-year limit for all restrictions and obligations. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information,' including trade secrets. The change introduces a direct conflict because it creates uncertainty about the duration of confidentiality obligations, specifically for trade secrets. The original text implies a five year limit on all obligations, while the changed text states that trade secrets have no limit. This creates an in-text contradiction.
